evals.prompt:
Can you reframe this for my VP? I'm sending it as a Slack message before our Monday sync.

---
Hi Sarah, wanted to give you a heads-up ahead of Monday. So we've been working on the checkout flow refactor since Q3 — the team has done a great job migrating the cart service to the new microservices architecture, and we finished the Redis caching layer last sprint. We still have 3 sprints left on the payment gateway integration, which is going well, and the team is on track. However, we ran into an issue last week: our third-party fraud detection provider (Sift) announced they're deprecating the v2 API that we're currently using, and the sunset date is March 31st. We had a call with their team on Thursday and they said migration to v3 requires a significant rework of our webhook handler and some changes to our risk-scoring logic. Engineering estimates it'll take 3–4 weeks of work. The thing is, this wasn't in the original project scope and we haven't planned for it. We're currently in the process of evaluating whether we should absorb this into the current sprint or push it into a separate workstream. I wanted to flag it for you because it might affect Q1 planning. Happy to discuss more on Monday.
---
evals.expected_output: A four-part exec frame (What matters / Why now / Risk if we do nothing / Recommendation) that surfaces the Sift API deprecation as the decision point, removes the implementation context about microservices and Redis, and ends with a clear ask for the VP

evals.prompt:
This is going into the exec summary of a steering committee deck next week. Can you make it tighter and more exec-ready?

---
The data platform modernization initiative was kicked off in January following the architecture review conducted in Q4 2024. Since then, the team has completed vendor evaluation (we assessed Snowflake, Databricks, and BigQuery across 12 evaluation criteria including cost, latency, ecosystem fit, and migration complexity), conducted 14 stakeholder interviews across Engineering, Analytics, Marketing, and Finance, and produced a 47-page technical assessment document. Based on this analysis, the recommendation is to migrate to Snowflake. The migration plan involves 4 phases spanning 18 months, with Phase 1 (foundation setup and pilot migration of 3 non-critical datasets) estimated at 6 weeks. Total estimated cost is $2.1M over 3 years, compared to current spend of $3.4M over the same horizon — a projected saving of $1.3M. The main risks identified are: (1) data pipeline re-engineering complexity, (2) analyst productivity during transition, and (3) vendor lock-in. The team is requesting steering committee approval to proceed to Phase 1.
---
evals.expected_output: A compressed exec frame that leads with the recommendation (migrate to Snowflake, $1.3M savings), states why now (steering committee decision needed to begin Phase 1), surfaces the real risk (not the full 3 risks but the most decision-relevant one), and ends with a crisp ask to approve Phase 1

evals.prompt:
I need to send this to the CTO. It's an email about our on-call situation. Right now it's a bit of a wall of text and I think it's going to get ignored. Help.

---
Subject: On-call update

Hi Marc,

I wanted to give you an update on the on-call situation. As you may know, we restructured our on-call rotation in October after the service outages we had in September. Since then, the team has been running a two-tier system where Tier 1 handles initial triage and Tier 2 handles escalations. Overall the system is working but we've been experiencing some pain points.

In the past 6 weeks, we've had 23 pages, of which 14 were during business hours and 9 were outside business hours (including 4 between midnight and 6am). Of the 9 off-hours pages, 7 required Tier 2 escalation, which means senior engineers are being woken up multiple times a week. We had two engineers tell me last week that they're considering leaving because of the on-call burden. One of them is our lead for the payments service, which is our most critical system.

I've been working with the team to identify the root causes and we've narrowed it down to three recurring issues: a noisy alerting configuration that fires too often on transient errors (this accounts for about 40% of pages), two services (order processing and notification dispatch) that don't have proper runbooks and escalate to Tier 2 by default, and a staffing gap — we don't have enough senior engineers in EMEA time zones to cover night pages without routing to US-based staff.

We have a proposal to fix the alerting configuration and write the runbooks (this can be done in 4–6 weeks and would reduce pages by an estimated 50–60%). The EMEA staffing gap is a bigger issue that requires a hiring decision.

I wanted to bring this to your attention because the alerting and runbook work we can self-organize, but the EMEA hiring decision is outside my scope to make alone.
---
evals.expected_output: An exec frame that isolates the two real issues (on-call burden is causing attrition risk on a critical service, and the EMEA hiring decision is blocked), strips the metrics and root-cause analysis, and asks the CTO for a specific decision on EMEA hiring
